element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Movement in four directions (horizontal/vertical) across empty floor squares
Basic push mechanic - player walks up to box and pushes it to adjacent empty square
Visual marker (red dot) showing destination for box placement
Obstacles that block player and box movement, define puzzle boundaries
Managing box sequencing; understanding that only one box can be pushed at a time
Learning to recognize permanent traps: boxes against walls, boxes in dead ends, blocked access patterns
Planning several moves ahead; considering all possible outcomes to avoid unsolvable states

typical_relief_triggers: Successfully placing a box on storage location
Undoing moves to recover from mistake
typical_tension_triggers: Realizing a careless move created a deadlock
Box positioned against wall or another box
Uncertainty about whether remaining boxes can reach storage locations
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Self-paced cognitive puzzle rhythm; tension builds through planning burden and deadlock awareness, releases upon successful placement or level completion; intensity depends on player's lookahead capability and puzzle complexity rather than time pressure

difficulty_curve.early_game: Introduction of core mechanics (movement, single box push, storage location placement) with safe, learnable scenarios; designed to teach push mechanics without deadlock risk
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Multiple boxes introduced; deadlock conditions become active puzzle obstacles; players must develop planning and foresight skills
difficulty_curve.late_game: Complex multi-step planning; high deadlock risk; intricate box sequencing; levels designed to be challenging even for skilled players
notable_spikes: Transition from single-box to multi-box puzzles (risk of permanent box trapping introduced)
Introduction of narrow corridors and dead-end configurations (spatial constraint complexity spike)
Late-stage puzzles requiring 10+ move lookahead planning

notable_level_patterns.description: Early levels feature open warehouse spaces with clear paths; subsequent levels add walls, narrow corridors, and dead ends to increase spatial complexity and deadlock risk
notable_level_patterns.example_level: First level of NEC PC-8801 version (documented as simple introductory level)

notable_level_patterns.description: Multiple boxes require careful ordering of placement; moving one box incorrectly can block access to remaining boxes or force suboptimal placement that creates deadlock
Spatial design where boxes can be pushed into alcoves or corridors with only one entrance/exit, creating permanent traps if boxes are placed there
